summary: Gruber addresses the controversy over Apple pushing Safari to Windows users through its Software Update utility. He argues that the real problem isn't whether Apple should offer Safari, but that the Software Update app's design fails to distinguish between updates to installed software and offers to install new software. He proposes that new software should be displayed separately with distinct visual treatment, with installation defaulting to off or requiring explicit user action. He concludes that Apple's laziness in not redesigning the update interface caused an entirely avoidable controversy.
key_points: The debate was polarized because people argued about the wrong thing — the real issue is Software Update's design, not whether Apple should offer Safari
Updates to installed software and offers for new software are fundamentally different things and should look visually distinct
New software offers should default to not installing, or require explicit user choice, with an easy option to permanently dismiss the offer
Apple likely skipped the redesign because Safari was the only new Windows app they planned to push, but laziness doesn't excuse bad design
money_quotes: The reason reactions to this controversy have been so polarized is that we've been mostly arguing about the wrong thing.
Different things should look different; the current design of Software Update doesn't allow for such a visual differentiation.
It should be clear that what's being offered is both new and optional.
Companies shouldn't abuse that to push entirely unrelated software on end users.
This entire controversy, minuscule though it may be, could — and should — have been avoided if Apple had followed the design principle of making things that are different look different.
key_insight: The Apple Software Update controversy was fundamentally a design failure — different things should look different, and conflating software updates with new software installations violates a basic design principle.
